title: Correcting Inconsistent Type Inference for a Generic Math Calculator

statement.markdown: Implement a generic math calculator function that can perform addition, subtraction, multiplication, and division based on the given operator. The function should work with both numbers and strings. However, the current implementation has inconsistent type inference.
statement.inputFormat: The function takes three parameters: `num1` and `num2` of type `T` and an operator of type `string`.
statement.outputFormat: The function returns the result of the operation as a `number` if the operation is one of the basic math operations, otherwise it returns the concatenated string.
statement.notes: The function should handle division by zero correctly and raise an error.

constraints.description: Maximum length of the input string

hints.text: Use generics to ensure type safety
Use a type guard to handle division by zero

editorial.title: Correcting Inconsistent Type Inference for a Generic Math Calculator
editorial.intuition: The problem requires us to use generics to ensure type safety and use a type guard to handle division by zero.
editorial.bruteForce: We can use a brute force approach by checking the type of each parameter and performing the operation accordingly.
editorial.betterSolution: We can use generics to ensure type safety and use a type guard to handle division by zero.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution uses generics to ensure type safety and uses a type guard to handle division by zero.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The proof of correctness involves showing that the solution handles all possible inputs and edge cases correctly.
editorial.dryRun: A dry run would involve testing the solution with various inputs to ensure it works as expected.

functionSignature.javascript: const calculate = (op: string, num1: any, num2: any) => any
